review_text: Listen, slow does not necessarily mean bad. I can handle slow movies. But I really don't know why this movie was made. I don't get the purpose of it. Is it just supposed to be about a friendship? I really don't think it went very deep about that. Is it supposed to be a critique of capitalism or something? Getting ahead requires a crime of some sort? Eh, spare me. You know from the beginning it ain't gonna end well, so it's just a draggy slog all the way through.

Director Kelly Reichardt has proven over the course of her career that she understands the fact that there is beauty in simplicity. In First Cow, set in early 19th-century Oregon, two seemingly disparate men (John Magaro, Orion Lee) steal milk from the only cow in the area in order to sweeten the rolls they sell in the local market, thus increasing their profits. It’s a simple story dealing with simple themes, shot in what appears to be natural light with subdued performances, lovely cinematography, patient editing and deliberate pacing. In an era where the American film landscape is filled with frantic remakes, sequels, and prequels, having more filmmakers like Kelly Reichardt would be refreshing.
Great script. Well acted. Nice period piece with a simple, but worthwhile plot.

The gentle pacing of First Cow is reminiscent of a nature documentary, which of course it is - a study of human nature on the frontier of the Pacific northwest, before electricity, gas and TV. As the frontier creeps towards the Pacific ocean the trappings of civilization gradually appear from the east, including the first cow to arrive in the settlement. The central characters are searching for opportunity in a land without a safety net, and it is perhaps this sense of insecurity that makes a simple story so compelling. Crafted with Kelly Reichardt's distinctive ability to find profound meaning in quiet and understated moments, this film is a refreshing contrast to the overabundance of overstuffed movies that lack both authenticity and humanity.

This is a slow, thoughtful, contemplative perhaps, film. Its quite relaxing in its both rural setting and the instrumental, sometimes banjo or other percussion instruments, music played lightly/sparingly in the background. It's quite quiet, dialogue wise but that's not a bad thing I felt. I enjoyed the cinematography - I like that its understated. Laidback is a term I'd definitely use to describe this film. I suppose its a nice depiction of male companionship to an extent and it highlights a form of isolation that the two main characters no doubt feel regardless. I thought the two main characters were well portrayed by John Magaro as Cookie and Orion Lee as King-Lu - I didn't really recognise anyone other than Toby Jones, who seems a nice enough guy at first (he plays the 'chief factor', who is unnamed). I'd say there is a bit of a moral aspect to it. Its not a film for people who like constant action and are hoping for a high octane type Western film but I would say its pretty atmospheric never the less and I definitely enjoyed it overall, very much so. By the end of the film, I felt myself hoping for the best for the two main characters, who are outcasts of sorts. Yes, I'd recommend this film, definitely.

A story about two outcasts who find friendship and a business partnership with each other in the American frontier. John Magaro plays a gentle baker who begins the movie as cook for a prospecting expedition. He's too thoughtful and sensitive to really fit in with the macho wild men he's with, who bully and threaten him. Orion Lee is a Chinese immigrant who befriends him and suggests that they go into business selling the baker's biscuits at a local trading post. The biscuits are a sensation, but the enterprise is a dangerous one -- the only place to get milk is to steal it from the cow (yes, THE cow, not A cow) that belongs to the local aristocrat, played by the always welcome Toby Jones. The film begins in the present day with a hiker in the woods discovering something that hints at how the movie will end, and how the movie then circles back to that find is a small miracle of narrative storytelling. Kelly Reichardt, who's made a career of quiet, understated films, remains true to form here. "First Cow" shows much more than it tells, which should please fans of art house fare but will probably frustrate more casual moviegoers who have less patience for the way stories are told. The two main characters don't talk much, but we learn much about them, not from what they say, but from how they act. The first time we see Magaro's character, for example, he's hunting for mushrooms in the woods, and he takes the time to set a salamander he finds back on its feet when it's struggling to flip itself over. And when Lee's character shows him to his cabin in the woods, the first thing Magaro does is find some wildflowers with which to decorate the small space. Little flourishes like these reward the patient viewer, and we come to like the two men so much that the end, though we've been warned of it in advance, feels that much more tragic when it comes.
Beautiful, hopeful, enjoyable but ends leaving the audience wanting a little more.
The first scene is inauthentic but the attention to detail, lush color and how the light in each scene is nontraditionally played with is a spectacle. A little slow in story but visually pleasing.

Although slow moving, it allows you to feel what it is like to live among the primitive white men of the West and how friendship give meaning to a hard existence. You will be thinking about this film long after it is over.

First Cow is superbly filmed and every performance nearly perfected, but classic status is withheld by a predictable story and criminally slow pacing to underline this fact. Lee and Magaro have quite a rapport and should have bright futures. Toby Jones is wonderful in a supporting role. The setting of the frontier at the very beginning of settlement brings an air of tranquility that helps distract from the long single shots and slow, pacified stretches of dialogue. First Cow's positives and feelgood yet suspenseful examination of timeless themes in a period setting outweigh its deficiencies.

When, when, WHEN am I going to learn? That if the first 50 minutes of a film is lousy, it just isn't going to get any better. Imagine an intelligent, uplifting movie that keeps you on the edge of your seat. Then imagine the opposite. 1-1/2 stars for the first-rate cinematography.

Beginning with a bafflingly long, stationary shot of a barge slowly sailing across the screen, Kelly Reichardt sets the tone and pace of her critically acclaimed film which stretches out and over-pads a simple and potentially elegant story of camaraderie and friendship between two lost souls in 1820s Oregon. The glacially-paced film takes it sweet time establishing the relationship between a timid cook who meets (cute) a buck-naked but well spoken Chinese immigrant on the run from some Russians but that background information is pretty much never bothered with again as soon as their bromance blossoms into a not-exactly-legal enterprise selling 'oily cakes' using stolen milk from the titular cow owned by Toby Jones' pompous Chief Factor. Taking its cues from Altman's McCabe & Mrs. Miller, the production design, the use of academy ratio and the look of the cast authentically evoke the era it's set in but even with the occasionally interesting cinematography thrown in, there really isn't that much to hold my attention, especially when her shots are more than often deliberately held just a little too long in an awkward, slightly jarring fashion. The inventive main story, like McCabe, suggests an allegory to the fundamental economic principles on which the United States was built on, but it's a thin one that cannot sustain the film's 2hrs duration, while there's clearly a less patience-testing and perfectly coherent 90mins film waiting to be found in the editing suite. It doesn't help that a contemporarily set prologue promises something that fails to pay off in the very indie/abrupt ending. There's obviously a niche audience for a film like this but I can't see the attraction other more positive reviewers see in this excessively lyrical, borderline indulgent film that I reckon many will find a chore to have to slog through.

First Cow is a quietly powerful ode to friendship. A film of great beauty, great humanity and strong empathy. It's a film of slow nature but one of huge impact. Kelly Reichardt is a very singular film maker indeed, her films are often slow and unrushed. They quietly take their time to build the story and I can see that her films are definitely not for everybody. If big Hollywood films are fast food, then her's are slow food. Good for the soul but not a rush to the head. Her latest film is set in the Oregon Territory in the 1820s. Cookie is a chef working for a band of trappers. He meets Lu, a Chinese man on the run. They form a bond and settle in a small outpost, dreaming of life beyond, a better one for both. Then the first milk cow in the territory enters the picture and they have an idea. They will 'borrow' the milk at night and then bake cakes to be sold, which prove very popular, making them enough money to pursue their dreams. But not all goes to plan. It's a simple narrative with the camera lingering on many everyday scenes. We stop by for conversations and observations and this is the heart of the film. The friendship formed is quite beautiful and shows how Reichardt is very adept at human connections. She also uses 4:3 aspect ratio which is basically like a square, giving the film more of a contained feeling. The cinematography is quite soft focus too aiding a gentler nature. Lead performances are great too, with Orion Lee and John Magaro perfectly suited to their roles. This film is precise and simple, a film of lovely charm and great impact. It's not for everyone but if you adjust to its pace the rewards are great indeed.

Watching First Cow, you nod at the craft thinking 'yeah, this is elevated' before you suddenly realize its premise (quite reminiscent of many Netflix evenings, if you ask me): I want biscuits, f*ck, I'd do anything for a biscuit about now. As Cookie and King Lu edge their way to a cow, every night, risking their lives to steal precious milk, you truly grow attached to them. First Cow starts with a brilliant quote by William Blake, and continues with long, milked (excuse the pun) still scenes. And while I expected to dislike the slow pace of the film, I find myself caught in a moment of mindfulness - the water laps onto the shore to the left, the clouds move to the right, the shadow of the trees dances on the ground. I feel quite at peace. The cinematic experience is very much 'sans thrills' (potentially the total opposite to Hollywoodian stereotypes: grainy visuals, still shots, slow scenes, limited music), and yet - the tension does grow nearer the end. Reichardt has a way of making you feel important as a viewer, by being very much *not* on the nose - notonthenose? She delivers a humble, effective visual spectacle and trusts you to make the links, understand the subtleties. It's almost a cinematic compliment. First Cow loses points for me, despite the mindfulness incurred, in a slow first half of the film. The satisfying cow-chase doesn't kick off for a while, and we don't get to delight in the efficient collision of baking and 19th century Northwest scenes for long. While I left with a smile on my lips, it took some initial commitment. Not sure, as well, if I'd pick to watch it again. Overall, First Cow felt like tasting a very expensive product - it startled me, made me feel special, and left me with a smile - but I won't revisit it.

It's not a movie for mainstream audiences. Its pacing is pretty slow and the ending is a little anticlimactic, even I felt it could've been about 15 minutes shorter. But I still thought First Cow was an interesting period drama that kept me engaged due to the performances and the friendship between the characters played by John Magaro and Orion Lee as well as the well-written script by Kelly Reichardt and Jonathan Raymond and it is very well-photographed by Christopher Blauvelt.

First Cow is a beautiful film to look at, with a refreshingly original plot and quirky, relatable characters. Personally I found this film to be a little too long and I felt like the set up for the film took up an unnecessary amount of it's run time. It's a smart film, but takes too long to get to its point which could unfortunately turn off some viewers. With that being said the final act of this film is brilliantly directed and executed with one of the strongest film endings of 2020.
I appreciate the artistic effort but this is the slowest of slow burns. It could have been a short film.

Kelly Reichardt's First Cow is, seemingly, an ordinary tale about ordinary people, following two unlikely friends start a business in an Oregon small community in the early 19th century. A kind, quiet cook, played by John Magaro, is traveling the country and looking for work. After helping him survive, Cookie, as is his nickname, befriends a Chinese man named King-Lu (Orion Lee), who offers the cook a home. From their sincere friendship, a business idea blossoms when the local baron played by Toby Jones brings one single cow to the land in order to supply his homestead with milk. The two actors in the center of the film nail their individual performances, creating believable and endearing characters, especially in quiet the moments that aren't essential to the plot, where Reichardt finds poetry in the mundane. It was a clever directorial decision to use a 4:3 aspect ratio and to set the story to the beautiful soundtrack of the nature for further immersion in those times and to emphasize the fact that First Cow may be small in scope, but thematically big and clinically careful in execution. At the end of the day, Reichardt created a riveting parable of few words, as enchanting through its patience as it is thought provoking through its ideas.

Tedious screed. Historically inaccurate. (Apparently the only thing that the frontier received from the East were rags, the film implies.). Feminist self-serving notions of male brutality . Clafouti is not a "gourmet" French dish - it's a pancake with fried fruit.

The people who made the film should be proud that they accomplished something that seems to align with their vision. The critics who gave the film high marks should be devastatingly ashamed of their asinine, and likely deluded, inability to understand what the vast vast majority of human beings think and feel in response to movies. Critics have, perhaps, the easiest job on the face of the planet - to consume entertainment and tell people what's good; and I am simply dumbfounded at their complete and utter incompetence, which quite frankly is much more easily explainable by willful failure. It's easy, for example, to imagine that many film critics are so deeply insecure that they grasp at any way of using the paltry power they have found in life to make themselves feel superior; in this example, by giving high marks to a movie that they know very few people will appreciate, so they can therefore lord their supposedly refined taste over others. Alternatively, I suppose it's possible that many critics are so truly bored of their own profession that watching a movie (which is barely a movie at all) is such a nice escape that they completely lose sight of just how detached they've allowed themselves to become. In any case, if for some tragic reason, you trust the critics on this one and put First Cow on when you could be occupying your time with something far more engaging (like staring in the mirror and watching yourself age), then I can only share with you, that as the movie opens on the discovery of two human skeletons, I promise you'll end up wishing they were the film critics who you trusted.
